content.text: GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) is the container format used by llama.cpp, the C/C++ LLM inference engine that made running large language models on consumer hardware mainstream. Introduced in August 2023 to replace the earlier GGML format, GGUF bundles model weights, quantization metadata, tokenizer vocabulary, and all configuration into a single self-contained binary file. This makes GGUF models trivially portable — one file contains everything needed to run inference on any GGUF-compatible runtime. Its K-quant quantization methods, jointly developed by the llama.cpp community, provide some of the best quality-per-byte ratios available, enabling models like Llama-3 70B to run locally on a laptop.
A GGUF file is a structured binary format designed for zero-copy memory-mapped loading. The layout is:

attrs.alt: GGUF file structure: magic header, key-value metadata (arch/vocab/context_length/tokenizer), tensor metadata array, tensor data blobs. K-quant comparison table: Q2_K through Q8_0, with Q4_K_M as recommended default. Hybrid CPU+GPU inference with n_gpu_layers splits layers across VRAM and RAM.

content.text: 4-byte magic 0x46554747 ('GGUF' in little-endian) + version number (currently 3). Parsers use this to detect the file type before reading anything else.

key-value pairs storing architecture name (llama, mistral, phi, etc.), vocab size, context length, number of layers, attention head counts, RoPE parameters, and the complete tokenizer (BPE merges + vocabulary). Every configuration value needed to instantiate the model is here — no separate tokenizer.json or config.json needed.

for each tensor: name (e.g., blk.3.attn_q.weight), shape, quantization type (Q4_K, Q6_K, F16, etc.), and byte offset into the tensor data section. The offset allows direct memory-mapped access to any tensor without parsing the entire file.
raw quantized bytes for all tensors, aligned to 32-byte boundaries for SIMD efficiency. Memory mapping this section gives instant zero-copy access on any platform.

# Reading GGUF metadata with gguf-reader (pip install gguf)
from gguf import GGUFReader

reader = GGUFReader("llama-2-7b-chat.Q4_K_M.gguf")

# Print all metadata
for field in reader.fields.values():
    print(f"{field.name}: {field.parts[-1]}")
# llama.architecture: llama
# llama.context_length: 4096
# llama.embedding_length: 4096
# llama.block_count: 32
# llama.attention.head_count: 32
# tokenizer.ggml.model: llama

# Inspect tensor metadata
for tensor in reader.tensors:
    print(f"{tensor.name}: shape={tensor.shape}, type={tensor.tensor_type.name}")
# token_embd.weight: shape=[32000, 4096], type=Q4_K
# blk.0.attn_norm.weight: shape=[4096], type=F32  (norms kept at F32)
# blk.0.attn_q.weight: shape=[4096, 4096], type=Q4_K
The K-quant family (Q2_K through Q6_K) uses a hierarchical block structure that differs from the flat per-group quantization of GPTQ/AWQ. Each quantization type has two levels of granularity:
a large block of weights (e.g., 256 weights for Q4_K). One FP16 super-block scale is stored per super-block.
a smaller block within the super-block (e.g., 32 weights for Q4_K). One quantised scale (stored as a low-bit integer) is stored per sub-block.

This two-level structure means the effective quantization uses locally-adapted scales while keeping memory overhead low. The notation Q{N}_K means N-bit weights with K-quant block structure; the _S/_M/_L suffixes indicate different sub-block configurations affecting the trade-off between super-block scale overhead and per-sub-block scale accuracy:

Unlike GPTQ and AWQ which minimise layer-wise output error using calibration data, K-quant quantization is calibration-free — it uses only the weight values themselves to compute scales. This makes conversion trivially fast (seconds per model) at the cost of slightly higher perplexity than calibration-based methods at the same bit-width.

The llama.cpp repository includes the conversion and quantization scripts. Converting a HuggingFace model to GGUF is a two-step process: first convert to GGUF F16 (full precision), then quantize to the desired K-quant type:

# Step 1: Clone llama.cpp and install Python dependencies
git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp
cd llama.cpp
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Build llama.cpp (requires CMake)
cmake -B build -DLLAMA_CUDA=ON   # -DLLAMA_CUDA=ON for GPU support
cmake --build build --config Release -j $(nproc)

# Step 2: Convert HuggingFace model to GGUF F16
# Assumes model is downloaded to ./llama-2-7b-hf/
python convert_hf_to_gguf.py ./llama-2-7b-hf/ \
    --outtype f16 \
    --outfile ./llama-2-7b-f16.gguf

# Step 3: Quantize F16 GGUF to Q4_K_M
./build/bin/llama-quantize \
    ./llama-2-7b-f16.gguf \
    ./llama-2-7b-Q4_K_M.gguf \
    Q4_K_M

# Other quantization types:
# Q3_K_M  → smaller, lower quality
# Q5_K_M  → larger, higher quality
# Q6_K    → nearly lossless
# Q8_0    → lossless for most purposes
Always start from a full-precision (F16 or BF16) HuggingFace checkpoint, not a previously quantized model. Re-quantizing a GPTQ or AWQ model through GGUF introduces compounding quantization error.
The llama.cpp CLI provides direct model execution with extensive configuration for context length, batch size, sampling parameters, and CPU/GPU split:

# Basic text completion
./build/bin/llama-cli \
    -m ./llama-2-7b-chat-Q4_K_M.gguf \
    -p "The capital of France is" \
    -n 100           # generate 100 tokens
    --temp 0.8       # temperature
    --top-p 0.95     # nucleus sampling

# GPU-accelerated inference (offload all layers to GPU)
./build/bin/llama-cli \
    -m ./llama-2-7b-chat-Q4_K_M.gguf \
    --n-gpu-layers 99   # offload all layers (use a large number)
    --ctx-size 4096     # context window
    -p "Explain GGUF quantization:" -n 500

# Hybrid CPU+GPU inference (split across 8 GB GPU + RAM)
./build/bin/llama-cli \
    -m ./llama-2-70b-chat-Q4_K_M.gguf \
    --n-gpu-layers 20    # first 20 layers on GPU
    --ctx-size 2048
    -p "Explain GGUF:" -n 200

# OpenAI-compatible server (used by LM Studio, Continue.dev, etc.)
./build/bin/llama-server \
    -m ./llama-2-7b-chat-Q4_K_M.gguf \
    --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 \
    --n-gpu-layers 99
# Access at: http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions
The llama-cpp-python package wraps llama.cpp with a Python API that mirrors the OpenAI interface, making it easy to use GGUF models from Python applications:

# pip install llama-cpp-python
# GPU support: CMAKE_ARGS="-DLLAMA_CUDA=on" pip install llama-cpp-python
from llama_cpp import Llama

# Load model — downloaded from HuggingFace Hub with GGUF files
llm = Llama(
    model_path="./llama-2-7b-chat-Q4_K_M.gguf",
    n_gpu_layers=-1,     # -1 = offload all layers to GPU
    n_ctx=4096,          # context window size
    n_threads=8,         # CPU threads for non-GPU layers
    verbose=False,
)

# Simple completion
output = llm(
    "Q: What is quantization? A:",
    max_tokens=256,
    temperature=0.0,
    stop=["Q:", "\n\n"],
)
print(output["choices"][0]["text"])

# Chat completion (OpenAI-compatible)
response = llm.create_chat_completion(
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain GGUF quantization in one paragraph."},
    ],
    temperature=0.0,
    max_tokens=512,
)
print(response["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])
Ollama: GGUF with a Model Registry
Ollama is a higher-level runtime built on top of llama.cpp that adds a model registry (Modelfile), automatic hardware detection, a REST API, and a CLI for pulling and running GGUF models:

# Install Ollama (Linux/macOS)
curl -fsSL https://ollama.ai/install.sh | sh

# Pull and run a quantized model from the Ollama registry
ollama run llama3          # auto-selects Q4_K_M for your hardware
ollama run llama3:70b      # 70B model (auto-selects quantization)
ollama run mistral:7b-instruct-q5_K_M  # explicit quant type

# Run your own GGUF file
cat > Modelfile <<'EOF'
FROM ./llama-2-7b-chat-Q4_K_M.gguf
SYSTEM "You are a helpful assistant."
PARAMETER temperature 0.7
PARAMETER num_ctx 4096
EOF
ollama create my-llama2 -f Modelfile
ollama run my-llama2

# Ollama REST API (runs when `ollama serve` is active)
import requests

response = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:11434/api/chat",
    json={
        "model": "llama3",
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Explain GGUF."}],
        "stream": False,
    },
)
print(response.json()["message"]["content"])

# Ollama also supports the OpenAI-compatible endpoint:
# POST http://localhost:11434/v1/chat/completions
The best quantization type depends on available VRAM and acceptable quality loss. For a 7B model:
Q4_K_M fits with ~1 GB headroom for context
Visually lossless; leaves room for large context
Effectively no quality loss; use F16 if fine-tuning

For a 13B model, shift all thresholds up by roughly 2×. For 70B models: Q4_K_M on 2× RTX 4090 (48 GB combined) or Q4_K_S on a single A100 40 GB. Hybrid CPU+GPU mode (n_gpu_layers < total layers) allows running 70B on systems with 8–16 GB VRAM + 32–48 GB RAM, at 3–8 tokens/sec.

GGUF is the format of choice for running models locally on consumer hardware — it supports CPU, hybrid CPU+GPU, Apple Silicon (Metal), Vulkan, and ROCm without requiring a CUDA GPU. For server-side GPU-only inference, GPTQ and AWQ provide better throughput.
Understanding the K-quant block layout explains why Q4_K_M typically outperforms flat Q4 quantization at the same bit-width. Consider Q4_K_M for a weight tensor with d_in=4096:
The tensor is divided into super-blocks of 256 weights. Each super-block is subdivided into 8 sub-blocks of 32 weights. The quantization hierarchy is:
Each sub-block has its own 6-bit scale (stored as an integer) — 8 scales per super-block.
Each super-block has one FP16 scale that multiplies all 8 sub-block scales.
The actual weight quantization uses 4 bits per weight within each sub-block.
The total storage per weight is approximately:

This is slightly more than flat Q4 (4 bits exactly), but the locally-adapted sub-block scales dramatically reduce quantization error for tensors with non-uniform weight distributions. The extra 0.25 bits buys a perplexity improvement of 0.2–0.5 points over flat Q4 at the same model size.

Q4_K_S uses the same structure but with fewer sub-blocks (4 instead of 8 per super-block), reducing the scale overhead to 4.06 bits/weight at the cost of slightly coarser local adaptation. The _S/_M distinction matters more for Q3 and Q5 than for Q4.

One of llama.cpp's most impactful features is full GPU acceleration on Apple Silicon via the Metal GPU API. MacBooks and Mac Studios with M1/M2/M3/M4 chips use unified memory (RAM and VRAM are the same pool), so a 32 GB MacBook Pro can hold a 7B model fully in the GPU's memory without any CPU offload:

# Build llama.cpp with Metal support (default on macOS)
cmake -B build -DLLAMA_METAL=ON
cmake --build build --config Release -j $(sysctl -n hw.logicalcpu)

# Run inference fully on Apple GPU (all layers in unified memory)
./build/bin/llama-cli \
    -m ./llama-3-8b-instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf \
    --n-gpu-layers 99 \
    --ctx-size 8192 \
    -p "<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>\n\nExplain GGUF.<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>\n\n" \
    -n 500

# Typical speeds on Apple Silicon (Metal):
# M1 Pro 16 GB: ~20 tok/s for Llama-3 8B Q4_K_M
# M2 Max 96 GB: ~35 tok/s for Llama-3 8B, ~8 tok/s for Llama-3 70B Q4_K_M
# M3 Ultra 192 GB: ~12 tok/s for Llama-3 70B Q4_K_M

Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture also enables a unique capability: running models that would exceed discrete GPU VRAM entirely in unified memory. A 192 GB M3 Ultra can run Llama-3 70B at Q5_K_M (≈43 GB) or even a 405B model at Q2_K (≈105 GB) — all in the same memory pool shared between CPU and GPU.

The following table gives reference perplexity numbers (wikitext-2 test set) and model sizes for Llama-2 7B across K-quant levels. These numbers are representative benchmarks from the llama.cpp community; exact values vary by model architecture:

These numbers hold approximately for other 7B-class models. For 13B and 70B models, the PPL delta at each quant type is typically 30–50% smaller — larger models are more robust to quantization. A Llama-2 70B at Q4_K_M has PPL delta of roughly +0.08, compared to +0.18 for the 7B version at the same quant type.

The HuggingFace Hub hosts thousands of pre-quantized GGUF models, so you rarely need to convert yourself. TheBloke (and increasingly the original model authors) publish Q2_K through Q8_0 variants for most popular models:

from huggingface_hub import hf_hub_download

# Download a specific GGUF quant variant
model_path = hf_hub_download(
    repo_id="TheBloke/Llama-2-7B-Chat-GGUF",
    filename="llama-2-7b-chat.Q4_K_M.gguf",
    local_dir="./models",
)
print(f"Downloaded to: {model_path}")

# Or list available files in a repo
from huggingface_hub import list_repo_files

files = list(list_repo_files("TheBloke/Llama-2-7B-Chat-GGUF"))
gguf_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith(".gguf")]
print("\n".join(gguf_files))
# llama-2-7b-chat.Q2_K.gguf
# llama-2-7b-chat.Q3_K_M.gguf
# llama-2-7b-chat.Q4_K_M.gguf    <- most commonly downloaded
# llama-2-7b-chat.Q5_K_M.gguf
# llama-2-7b-chat.Q6_K.gguf
# llama-2-7b-chat.Q8_0.gguf

# Alternatively, use the huggingface-cli
pip install huggingface_hub[cli]

# Download a single file
huggingface-cli download TheBloke/Llama-2-7B-Chat-GGUF \
    llama-2-7b-chat.Q4_K_M.gguf \
    --local-dir ./models

# Or use ollama pull which handles GGUF download automatically
ollama pull llama2:7b-chat-q4_K_M
is a self-contained binary format storing weights, quantization metadata, and tokenizer in one file. Zero-copy memory mapping enables instant loading.
(Q2_K through Q6_K) use a two-level block structure with super-block and sub-block scales. No calibration data is needed — conversion takes seconds.
is the recommended default: 4.85 bits/weight, ~4.8 GB for a 7B model, perplexity within 0.15–0.2 of FP16, fits on an 8 GB GPU.
supports CUDA, Metal, Vulkan, and AVX2/AVX512 CPU inference, plus hybrid CPU+GPU mode via n_gpu_layers — enabling 70B models on consumer laptops.
provide higher-level interfaces with model registries and OpenAI-compatible APIs for easy integration with existing tooling.
GGUF wins for local/CPU/hybrid inference; GPTQ and AWQ win for high-throughput GPU serving in the PyTorch ecosystem.
